item.title: Image Removed
item.content:encoded: Image was removed due to not having clear permission to use the image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. I intended the [REDACTED] to say something like "The conversion of so and so remains the likeliest source". In retrospect, your version is better; I may adjust it.

I disagree with the bit about too many "and"s. Pacemakers and deep-brain stimulators are listed separately, as they are a separate "category" of item. I wanted to drive the point home that this thing replaces everything, whether malign or benign, as long as it is foreign and does not belong in the body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an interesting and fun (in a disgusting horror show way) scip but there are a few issues with the grammar.

In the penultimate sentence of paragraph 2 of the description the line "It is unknown how SCP-1300 restocks its pharmaceuticals, but given its method of operation, the conversion of [REDACTED] the likeliest source." I believe an 'is' after [REDACTED] may be needed.

In the third paragraph the line "These include transplanted organs, malign or benign tumors, lodged objects, pacemakers and deep-brain stimulators, and false teeth." There seems to be one too many 'ands' as it should probably read "These include transplanted organs, malign or benign tumors, lodged objects, pacemakers, deep-brain stimulators and false teeth."

+1 from me regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. Its a valid question about a procedure the Foundation would be sure to try and I think it should be addressed in the article. Good work though.

item.title: Red Slurry
item.content:encoded: I like this. It's got a good solid idea, but I feel like it could be built upon. About beta state, I love the idea of the SCP needing to "restock" at times and using beta state to scoop up more viable organic material. I also understand that it would try to replace the missing space. I just find "disorganized organic molecules" a little vague and it seems odd that more would not have been done to research this substance. If it is indeed waste material, maybe a note about finding traces of DNA or matter from past test subjects in the slurry?

item.title: Re: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: Good to hear! You can check our Image Policy for more info.

item.title: Re: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: Cool, are we doing this now? I'll make sure to attribute any other images I use.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
Photo taken by urban explorer Motts, whose work can be found here:

http://opacity.us/image3953_dentist_chair.htm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting, but this could be alot cooler with some backstory about how/where they got it. I'd love to read something about it being in a hospital or lab as a miracle device but then suddenly something goes wrong.

item.title: Creep and complete
item.content:encoded: I like this one. Not only it's the right amount of creepy (that, or I've a thing for machines that COULD help you or COULD murder you apropos of nothing), but it answers all the questions that rises. Sure, we don't know who made it and how it works, but it lack the "We don't care, just put in a storage room until someone trips on it" vibe I found on other items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fake boobs? Would it remove a breast implant to replaced it with a biological filling that is more compatible with the host and totally undetectable? It's a gold mine waiting to be exploited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a) ayup
b) ayup

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does it recycle the matter it takes in during beta mode into the fluid it uses to replace foreign materials?

Also, facial implants and piercings?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this was the first one i read when o came to the site and i've been tryng to find it to upvote it since I joined.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this thing do abortions?

item.title: Re: Being massively pedantic
item.content:encoded:
That's already the case — the relevant information is [REDACTED] out, and even the researchers aren't 100% sure — they only suspect that DNA examination is one way this SCP does what it does. There are no definite answers.

But thank you for the info anyway. All my scientific knowledge comes from wikipedia, so all corrections/advices are welcome.

item.title: Being massively pedantic
item.content:encoded: Whilst what you said is technically true, a cancer can be caused by as little as 2 single base point mutations in some cases, making it basically the same as host tissue. A cell may also contain more than 100 point mutation and still operate the same as normal, it all depends on the exact locations on the genome. i mean you could simply say its done though an unknown means, 'cos ya know, its an SCP so that works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They will not have the same DNA as the host. The reason they are growing abnormally is because of a mutation in cell DNA. Normally a cell will try to repair any DNA damage done to it, but if it divides before it can do so, the mutation is passed on to the next cell. That next cell begins dividing uncontrollably due to the mutation it carried from the parent cell — it considers its DNA proper at that point and will not attempt to repair it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well written and creepy! The fact that I have a phobia of needles and surgical tubes only makes it better for me.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but one minor point—a tumour is not a foreign object. They are merely human cells growing abnormally and will have the same DNA as the host.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much improved!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'll do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you wanted it specifically related to that, I would change it to, "In light of [DATA EXPUNGED] all testing…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the old version, but I like this one more. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neither hate it nor love it. Well written, but no vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does it not work as suggesting something has happened when testing occurred with brain damaged subjects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[DATA EXPUNGED] All testing with brain-damaged subjects is prohibited.

There's no reason this expungement needs to be here. Other than that upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. They will be replaced with ones created by SCP-1300. I'll add a note acknowledging this in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this mean that it will also remove and replace transplanted organs, as they are foreign to the body being operated on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you've clarified that. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm very glad you liked it! But no vote? :c

Anyway, I was aware of the similarity while I was writing the original version of this SCP. It does not bother me since the two are distinct enough to be considered entirely separate SCPs: 212 improves while 1300 only heals, 212 uses implants and cybernetics, 1300 only uses biological tissues, 212 can and does put new objects into the person's body while 1300 actively removes them and is incapable of doing anything but that… in many ways, 1300 is anti-212! haha, I just noticed that myself, too

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's getting there, but I did like the old cancer-gobbling couch version too. I think the language needs to be clearer and more specific with regards to the technical process of the surgery. I also think that the section of containment procedure regarding cancer patients could be moved to an addendum at the end of the article and have slightly less clunky wording.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Updated version is upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did thouroghly enjoy this (Mostly for the fact that it is one of the few SCP articles I've read which caused me place myself in the position of the victim subject), but I can only assume that, since this is a rewrite, similiarities to The Improver have already been brought to your attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Re-written old version to be more consistent in its effects.

Thanks VAElynx and Reject for their suggestions; special thanks to Dexanote for helping me edit and trim down the article. You're awesome, guys!
